volumeTitle: Children of Heracles. Hippolytus. Andromache. Hecuba
label: Children of Heracles (Section 333 - Section 550)

sections.label: Section 333
sections.original: σοί τʼ εὖ λέλεκται , καὶ τὰ τῶνδʼ αὐχῶ , γέρον ,
sections.translation: Thou hast spoken well , and I feel confident their conduct will be such ; our kindness will they not forget .

sections.label: Section 335
sections.original: κἀγὼ μὲν ἀστῶν σύλλογον ποήσομαι ,
sections.translation: Now will I muster the citizens and set them in array , that I may receive Mycenae ’ s host with serried ranks . But first will I send scouts to meet them , lest they fall upon me unawares ; for at Argos every man is prompt to answer to the call ,

sections.label: Section 340
sections.original: μάντεις δʼ ἀθροίσας θύσομαι . σὺ δʼ ἐς δόμους
sections.translation: and I will assemble prophets and ordain a sacrifice . But do thou leave the altar of Zeus and go with the children into the house ; for there are those , who will care for thee , even though I be abroad . Enter then my house , old man .

sections.label: Section 344
sections.original: οὐκ ἂν λίποιμι βωμόν · ἑζώμεσθα δὴ
sections.translation: I will not leave the altar . Let [*]

sections.label: Section 345
sections.original: ἱκέται μένοντες ἐνθάδʼ εὖ πρᾶξαι πόλιν ·
sections.translation: us sit here still , praying for the city ’ s fair success , and when thou hast made a glorious end of this struggle , will we go unto the house ; nor are the gods who champion us weaker than the gods of Argos , O king ; Hera , wife of Zeus , is their leader ;

sections.label: Section 350
sections.original: ἡμῶν δʼ Ἀθηνᾶ . φημὶ δʼ εἰς εὐπραξίαν
sections.translation: Athena ours . And this I say is an omen of success , that we have the stronger deity , for Pallas will not brook defeat . [ Exit Demophon .

sections.label: Section 1.353
sections.original: Εἰ σὺ μέγʼ αὐχεῖς , ἕτεροι
sections.translation: Though loud thy boasts , there be others care no more for thee for that ,

sections.label: Section 1.355
sections.original: ὦ ξεῖν ’ Ἀργόθεν ἐλθών ,
sections.translation: O stranger [*] from the land of Argos ; nor wilt thou scare my soul with swelling words . Not yet be this the fate of mighty Athens , beauteous town !

sections.label: Section 1.360
sections.original: ναις εἴη · σὺ δʼ ἄφρων , ὅ τʼ Ἄρ-
sections.translation: But thou art void of sense , and so is he , who lords it o ’ er Argos , the son of Sthenelus ,

sections.label: Section 1.362
sections.original: ὃς πόλιν ἐλθὼν ἑτέραν
sections.translation: — thou that comest to another state , in no wise weaker than Argos ,

sections.label: Section 1.365
sections.original: καὶ ἐμᾶς χθονὸς ἀντομένους
sections.translation: and , stranger that thou art , wouldst drag away by force suppliants of the gods , wanderers that cling [*] to my land for help , refusing to yield to our king , nor yet having any honest plea to urge . How can such conduct count as honourable ,

sections.label: Section 1.370
sections.original: η παρά γʼ εὖ φρονοῦσιν ;
sections.translation: at least in wise men ’ s judgment ?

sections.label: Section 371
sections.original: Εἰρήνα μὲν ἐμοί γʼ ἀρέ-
sections.translation: I am for peace myself ; yet I tell thee , wicked king , although thou come unto my city , thou shalt not get so easily what thou expectest .

sections.label: Section 375
sections.original: σεις · οὐ σοὶ μόνῳ ἔγχος οὐδʼ
sections.translation: Thou art not the only man to wield a sword or targe with plates of brass . Nay , thou eager warrior , I warn thee , bring not war ’ s alarms against our lovely

sections.label: Section 380
sections.original: πόλιν , ἀλλʼ ἀνάσχου .
sections.translation: town ; restrain thyself .

sections.label: Section 381
sections.original: ὦ παῖ , τί μοι σύννοιαν ὄμμασιν φέρων
sections.translation: My son , why , prithee , art thou returned with that anxious look ? Hast thou news of the enemy ? Are they coming , are they here , or what thy tidings ? For of a surety yon herald will not play us false .

sections.label: Section 385
sections.original: ὁ γὰρ στρατηγὸς εὐτυχὴς τὰ πρὸς θεῶν
sections.translation: No ! sure I am their captain , prosperous heretofore , will come , with thoughts exceeding proud against Athens . But Zeus doth punish overweening pride .

sections.label: Section 389
sections.original: ἥκει στράτευμʼ Ἀργεῖον Εὐρυσθεύς τʼ ἄναξ ·
sections.translation: The host of Argos is come , and Eurystheus its king ;

sections.label: Section 390
sections.original: ἐγώ νιν αὐτὸς εἶδον . ἄνδρα γὰρ χρεών ,
sections.translation: my own eyes saw him , for the man who thinks he knows good generalship must see the foe not by messengers alone . As yet , however , he hath not sent his host into the plain , but , camped upon a rocky brow ,

sections.label: Section 395
sections.original: σκοπεῖ — δόκησιν δὴ τόδʼ ἂν λέγοιμί σοι —
sections.translation: is watching — I only tell thee what I think this means — to see by which road to lead his army hither without fighting , and how to take up a safe position in this land . However , all my plans are by this time carefully laid ; the city is under arms , the victims

sections.label: Section 400
sections.original: ἕστηκεν οἷς χρὴ ταῦτα τέμνεσθαι θεῶν ,
sections.translation: stand ready to be slain to every god , whose due this is ; my seers have filled the town with sacrifices , to turn the foe to flight and keep our country safe . All those who chant prophetic words have I assembled , and have examined ancient oracles , both public and secret ,

sections.label: Section 405
sections.original: λόγια παλαιά , τῇδε γῇ σωτήρια .
sections.translation: as means to save this city . And though the several answers differ in many points , yet in one is the sentiment of all clearly the same ; they bid me sacrifice to Demeter ’ s [*] daughter some maiden from a noble father sprung .

sections.label: Section 410
sections.original: ἐγὼ δʼ ἔχω μέν , ὡς ὁρᾷς , προθυμίαν
sections.translation: Now I , though in your cause I am as zealous as thou seest , yet will not slay my child , nor will I compel any of my subjects to do so against his will ; for who of his own will doth harbour such an evil thought as to yield with his own hands the child he loves ?

sections.label: Section 415
sections.original: καὶ νῦν πικρὰς ἂν συστάσεις ἂν εἰσίδοις ,
sections.translation: And now thou mayest [*] see angry gatherings , where some declare , ’ tis right to stand by suppliant strangers , while others charge me with folly ; but if I do [*] this deed , a civil war is then and there on foot .

sections.label: Section 420
sections.original: ταῦτʼ οὖν ὅρα σὺ καὶ συνεξεύρισχʼ ὅπως
sections.translation: Do thou then look to this and help to find a way to save yourselves and this country without causing me to be slandered by the citizens . For I am no despot like a barbarian monarch ; but provided [*] I do what is just , just will my treatment be .

sections.label: Section 425
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ἦ πρόθυμον οὖσαν οὐκ ἐᾷ θεὸς
sections.translation: Can it be that heaven forbids this city to help strangers , when it hath the will and longing so to do ?

sections.label: Section 427
sections.original: ὦ τέκνʼ , ἔοιγμεν ναυτίλοισιν , οἵτινες
sections.translation: My children , we are even as those mariners , who have escaped the storm ’ s relentless rage , and have the land almost within their reach , but after all are driven back from shore by tempests to the deep again .

sections.label: Section 430
sections.original: πνοιαῖσιν ἠλάθησαν ἐς πόντον πάλιν .
sections.translation: Even so we , just as we reach the shore in seeming safety , are being thrust back from this land . Ah me ! Why , cruel hope , didst thou then cheer my heart , though thou didst not mean to make the boon complete ?

sections.label: Section 435
sections.original: συγγνωστὰ γάρ τοι καὶ τὰ τοῦδʼ , εἰ μὴ θέλει
sections.translation: The king may well be pardoned , if he will not slay his subjects ’ children ; and with my treatment here I am content ; if indeed ’ tis heaven ’ s will , I thus should fare , still is my gratitude to thee in no wise lost . Children , I know not what to do for you .

sections.label: Section 440
sections.original: ποῖ τρεψόμεσθα ; τίς γὰρ ἄστεπτος θεῶν ;
sections.translation: Whither shall we turn ? for what god ’ s altar have we left uncrowned ? to what fenced city have we failed to go ? Ruin and surrender are our instant lot , poor children ! If I must die , ’ tis naught to me , save that thereby I give those foes of mine some cause for joy .

sections.label: Section 445
sections.original: ὑμᾶς δὲ κλαίω καὶ κατοικτίρω , τέκνα ,
sections.translation: But you , children , I lament and pity , and that aged mother of your sire , Alcmena . Ah , woe is thee for thy long span of life ! and woe is me for all my idle toil ! ’ Twas after all our destined doom to fall into the hands of our hated foe ,

sections.label: Section 450
sections.original: πεσόντας αἰσχρῶς καὶ κακῶς λιπεῖν βίον .
sections.translation: and die a death of shame and misery . But lend me thine aid , thou knowest how ; for all hope of these children ’ s safety has not yet left me . Give me up instead of them to the Argives , O king ; run no risk , but let me save

sections.label: Section 455
sections.original: τέκνʼ · οὐ φιλεῖν δεῖ τὴν ἐμὴν ψυχήν · ἴτω .
sections.translation: the children ; to love my life becomes me not ; let it pass . Me [*] will Eurystheus be most glad to take and treat despitefully , as I was Heracles ’ companion ; for the man is but a boor ; wherefore wise men ought to pray to get a wise man for their foe , and not a proud senseless fool ;

sections.label: Section 460
sections.original: πολλῆς γὰρ αἰδοῦς καὶ δίκης τις ἂν τύχοι .
sections.translation: for so , even if by fortune [*] flouted , one would meet with much consideration .

sections.label: Section 461
sections.original: ὦ πρέσβυ , μή νυν τήνδʼ ἐπαιτιῶ πόλιν ·
sections.translation: Old man , blame [*] not this city ; for though perhaps a gain [*] to us , yet would it be a foul reproach that we betrayed strangers .

sections.label: Section 464
sections.original: γενναῖα μὲν τάδʼ εἶπας , ἀλλʼ ἀμήχανα .
sections.translation: A generous scheme is thine , but impossible .

sections.label: Section 465
sections.original: οὐ σοῦ χατίζων δεῦρʼ ἄναξ στρατηλατεῖ ·
sections.translation: ’ Tis not in quest of thee yon king comes marching hither ; what would Eurystheus gain by the death of one so old ? Nay , ’ tis these children ’ s blood he wants . For there is danger to a foe in the youthful scions of a noble race , whose memory dwells upon their father ’ s wrongs ;

sections.label: Section 470
sections.original: λύμας · ἃ κεῖνον πάντα προσκοπεῖν χρεών .
sections.translation: all this Eurystheus must foresee . But if thou hast any scheme besides , that better suits the time , be ready with it , for , since I heard that oracle , I am at a loss and full of fear . [ Exit Demophon .

sections.label: Section 474
sections.original: ξένοι , θράσος μοι μηδὲν ἐξόδοις ἐμαῖς
sections.translation: Sirs , impute not boldness to me , because

sections.label: Section 475
sections.original: προσθῆτε · πρῶτον γὰρ τόδʼ ἐξαιτήσομαι ·
sections.translation: I venture forth ; this shall be my first request , for a woman ’ s fairest crown is this , to practise silence and discretion , and abide at home in peace . But when I heard thy lamentations , Iolaus , I came forth , albeit I was not appointed to take the lead in my family .

sections.label: Section 480
sections.original: ἀλλʼ , εἰμὶ γάρ πως πρόσφορος , μέλει δέ μοι
sections.translation: Still in some sense am I fit to do so , for these my brothers are my chiefest care , and I fain would ask , as touching myself , whether some new trouble , added to the former woes , is gnawing at thy heart .

sections.label: Section 484
sections.original: ὦ παῖ , μάλιστα σʼ οὐ νεωστὶ δὴ τέκνων
sections.translation: My daughter , ’ tis nothing new that I should praise thee , as I justly may ,

sections.label: Section 485
sections.original: τῶν Ἡρακλείων ἐνδίκως αἰνεῖν ἔχω .
sections.translation: above all the children of Heracles . Our house seemed to be prospering , when back it fell again into a hopeless state ; for the king declares the prophets signify that he must order the sacrifice , not of bull or heifer , but of some tender maid

sections.label: Section 490
sections.original: σφάξαι Κόρῃ Δήμητρος ἥτις εὐγενής ,
sections.translation: of noble lineage , if we and this city are to exist . Herein is our perplexity ; the king refuses either to sacrifice his own or any other ’ s child . [*] Wherefore , though he use not terms express , yet doth he hint ,

sections.label: Section 495
sections.original: εἰ μή τι τούτων ἐξαμηχανήσομεν ,
sections.translation: that , unless we find some way out of this perplexity , we must seek some other land , for he this country fain would save .

sections.label: Section 498
sections.original: ἐν τῷδε κἀχόμεσθα σωθῆναι λόγῳ ;
sections.translation: Are these indeed the terms on which our safety depends ?

sections.label: Section 499
sections.original: ἐν τῷδε , τἄλλα γʼ εὐτυχῶς πεπραγότες .
sections.translation: Yea , on these ; if , that is , we are successful otherwise .

sections.label: Section 500
sections.original: μή νυν τρέσῃς ἔτʼ ἐχθρὸν Ἀργεῖον δόρυ ·
sections.translation: No longer then cower before the hated Argive spear ; for I , of my own free will , or ever they bid me , am ready to die and offer myself as a victim . For what excuse have we , if , while this city deems it right to incur a great danger on our behalf ,

sections.label: Section 505
sections.original: αὐτοὶ δὲ προστιθέντες ἄλλοισιν πόνους ,
sections.translation: we , though we might save ourselves , fly from death , by foisting our trouble on others ? No ! indeed , ’ twere surely most ridiculous to sit and mourn as suppliants of the gods , and show ourselves but cowards , children as we are of that illustrious sire .

sections.label: Section 510
sections.original: κακοὺς ὁρᾶσθαι · ποῦ τάδʼ ἐν χρηστοῖς πρέπει ;
sections.translation: Where among the brave is such conduct seen ? Better , I suppose , this city should be taken and I ( which Heaven forefend !) fall into the hands of the enemy , and then , for all I am my noble father ’ s child , meet an awful doom , and face the Death-god none the less .

sections.label: Section 515
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ἐκπεσοῦσα τῆσδʼ ἀλητεύσω χθονός ;
sections.translation: Shall I wander as an exile from this land ? Shall I not feel shame then , when someone says , as say they will , Why are ye come hither with suppliant boughs , loving your lives too well ? Begone from our land ! for we will not succour cowards .

sections.label: Section 520
sections.original: ἀλλʼ οὐδὲ μέντοι , τῶνδε μὲν τεθνηκότων ,
sections.translation: Nay , if these be slain and I alone be saved , I have no hope in any wise of being happy , though many ere now have in this hope betrayed their friends . For who will care to wed a lonely maid or make me mother of his children ?

sections.label: Section 525
sections.original: οὐκ οὖν θανεῖν ἄμεινον ἢ τούτων τυχεῖν
sections.translation: ’ Tis better I should die than meet such treatment , [*] little as I merit it . This were fitter treatment for some other , one that is not born to fame as I am . Conduct me to the scene of death , [*] crown me with garlands , and begin the rites , if so it please you ;

sections.label: Section 530
sections.original: νικᾶτε δʼ ἐχθρούς · ἥδε γὰρ ψυχὴ πάρα
sections.translation: then be victorious o ’ er the foe , for here I offer my life freely and without constraint , and for my brothers and myself I undertake to die . [*] For I , by loving not my life too well , have found a treasure very fair , a glorious means to leave it .

sections.label: Section 535
sections.original: φεῦ φεῦ , τί λέξω παρθένου μέγαν λόγον
sections.translation: Ah , what shall I say on hearing the maid ’ s brave words , she that is ready to die for her brothers ? Who can speak more noble words or do more noble deeds henceforth for ever ?

sections.label: Section 539
sections.original: ὦ τέκνον , οὐκ ἔστʼ ἄλλοθεν τὸ σὸν κάρα ,
sections.translation: Daughter , thou art his own true child , no other man ’ s but Heracles ’,

sections.label: Section 540
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ἐξ ἐκείνου σπέρμα τῆς θείας φρενὸς
sections.translation: that godlike soul ; proud am I of thy words , though I sorrow for thy lot . Yet will I propose a fairer method : ’ tis right to summon hither all the sisters of this maiden ,

sections.label: Section 545
sections.original: κᾆθʼ ἡ λαχοῦσα θνῃσκέτω γένους ὕπερ ·
sections.translation: and then let her , on whom the lot shall fall , die for her family ; for that thou shouldst die without the lot is not just .

sections.label: Section 547
sections.original: οὐκ ἂν θάνοιμι τῇ τύχῃ λαχοῦσʼ ἐγώ ·
sections.translation: My death shall no chance lot decide ; there is no graciousness in that ; peace ! old friend . But if ye accept and will avail you

sections.label: Section 550
sections.original: χρῆσθαι προθύμως , τὴν ἐμὴν ψυχὴν ἐγὼ
sections.translation: of my readiness , freely do I offer my life for these , and without constraint .
